desc:
At this dark and silent junction three ways meet. A hard-surfaced road from the
west curves northward and is met by a corridor coming from the east. There is
little ornamental carving here, though there is a sconce in the southern wall
which used to light this intersection. The air is pervaded by an odour of body
waste.
name: Deep Corridor

desc:
This is a corridor in part of the Fifth Deep of ruined Khazad-dum which has
somehow survived the convulsions that followed the release of Durin's Bane. The
doors along it seem to lead mainly to once-comfortable Dwarven residences. The
pavement is polished, though not inlaid with marble. A layer of dust has been
disturbed by footprints - maybe a century ago, maybe more recently.
name: Deep Corridor

desc:
This is a Dwarven residence, simple but not shabby. In essence it is a tunnel
delved northward into the stone to a depth of about 60 feet and broken up into
three areas by partitions. At the northern end is a broad niche which must have
served as a wall-bed. There is ornamental carving in a band along the walls
about eye level (for a Dwarf). Everything movable, however, has been looted from
here long ago.
name: Dwarven Home

desc:
A modest north-south corridor has been severed here by a great crack or fault in
the mountain's stone foundations. The fissure is too wide to cross, especially
as the lip of the passage opposite seems to have mostly collapsed into the
abyss. Only darkness can be seen above and below.
name: Passage

desc:
A great crack in the stone of the mountain's foundation runs east and west here,
rising high above your head and descending deep below your feet into the
blackness. A whiff of warm, pungent air arises from below. You support yourself
by wedging your body between the north and south walls of the fissure and must
take care to avoid either being stuck between them or (where they are farther
apart) plummeting down to search for the great crevice's bottom.
name: Fissure

desc:
Vertical fissures lead east, west, and north from this location. Formed in some
great subterranean earthquake, each is barely wide enough to pass through but
rises far overhead and has no bottom you can see. You must support yourself by
using the tiniest footholds and by wedging yourself between the walls. One must
pick one's way carefully between the irregular walls, avoiding places where the
crevice becomes too narrow to pass, forcing one to laboriously retrace one's
steps.
name: Fissure

desc:
You are supporting yourself between the vertical walls of a fissure which cuts
through the rock of the mountain in a north-south direction. The rock of the
walls is a drab grey. There is a dank aroma in the air here.
name: Fissure

desc:
You are travelling in a vertical fissure which runs east and west here. Above
you it disappears in unclimbable blackness; below it sheerly descends farther
than you can make out. You must support yourself on handholds and footholds in
the fissure's walls, as there is no path or indeed any horizontal surface. The
fissure is quite narrow, so even if you were to fall there is little danger of
your plummeting to your death. No, the likelihood is that you would be wedged
between the fissure's walls, there to die of thirst or suffocation.
name: Fissure

desc:
You are in a wider part of a crevice which travels upward from here at a steep
southward slant. Below and to the west the crevice diminishes to a crack, large
enough, perhaps, to house rats. There is little to see, as whichever way you go
your face is virtually pressed into the rock wall.
name: Slanting Crevice

desc:
A hard-surfaced road ascending from the east here meets a more ornately
decorated ceremonial road from the west. To the north is a broad gateway which
is sealed by immense bronze doors. The carving on the doors has been marred and
gouged; you gather that it was engraved with scenes of battle, but cannot make
out much more.
name: Sealed Junction

desc:
This is a point where three handsome arched passageways meet, each with its own
great bronze door. The stones of the arches are decorated with a rayed pattern,
and the floor is paved with exceptionally large polished slabs of pink and white
marble.
name: Passage Junction

desc:
A broad stairway whose treads are paved in a very lustrous black stone descends
into this broad room from above and to the west. The stair is cluttered with
stone, however, and a glance reveals that the stairway itself is completely
choked with large stones, some of which seem imminently ready to break loose,
roll down the stair, and crush anyone beneath. The walls of the room are
decorated with false columns, and there are archways to the north and south with
great bronze doors.
name: Collapsed Stairwell

desc:
Here the east-west road widens and flattens, becoming a plain underground
chamber. A curtain of chains is raised above the eastern exit; if it were
lowered, it would effectively obstruct passage on the road. To the north, a
stall can be seen containing the windlass which operated the curtain. There is a
metal hatch on the floor of the stall, and there are benches along the sides of
the chamber where porters and haulers would wait while their cargoes were being
inspected and tallied.
name: Abandoned Checkpoint

desc:
An underground road surfaced in a smooth black stone runs east and west here.
The walls of the passage are not ornamented, and the vaulted ceiling is dark and
unreflective. There is a light coating of dust on the floor, which billows up in
black acrid clouds as you pass; it resembles ash more than ordinary dust.
name: Hard-surfaced Road

desc:
The sloping tunnel curves here, downward to the east, upward to the north. A
rivulet of filthy water flows along the inside of the bend. The air here is hot
and acrid; numerous smells mingle, not least the stench of waste associated with
the foul stream and its cargo, but also the aromas of smoke, of rot, of burned
things. At the outside of the curve, a narrow metal ladder ascends.
name: Sloping Passage

desc:
Here the passage descending from the west becomes a reeking cesspool. A
makeshift dam of stone no more than a foot high has been built to block off the
rivulet from above and divert its filthy water into a northward-leading tunnel.
Above the dam the water has deposited its load of ill-smelling muck. There are
few places for lamps along the passage, and it must never have been really
bright; but now it is very black indeed. The walls are stained with soot and
seem to defeat the power of vision.
name: Muck-filled Passage

desc:
This was once an armoury in the days when dwarves dwelt in peace here. The dirty
stream which has been diverted into this room from the south has formed a vile
pool here. There is apparently a drain in the floor, and you hear water trickle
and splash far below you, but it must be largely clogged by sludge. Some broken
and rusted metal pieces lie rotting in the water; any valuable items were taken
from here ages ago. There are some shapeless pits in the muck, resembling
footprints.
name: Flooded Armoury

desc:
This welter of gigantic stones seems to go on without end. Every irregular
junction looks different when seen from a slightly different angle. There is no
way to know whether you have been near this spot before, much less what
direction you are facing. All walls, all veins, all surfaces, all that could
give some some semblance of order to your travel has been broken, jumbled,
disordered. The air is warm, dry, and smells faintly foul; as you move, you stir
up a stinking dust with an acrid, animalistic scent.
name: Devastation

desc:
You are in a chaotic jumble of rock masses formed long ago in the almost
complete destruction of Khazad-dum's Fourth Deep. In a terrible series of
shudderings of the earth, almost the entire ceiling of the deep collapsed in a
single instant, smashing flat all the homes, storerooms, and cellars that were
therein. Into the cavity where the rock of the ceiling had been, more rock fell.
Now all is smashed up together here - natural stone and cut stone alike, in a
lifeless and patternless heap. Your sense of direction is useless here. Some
crevices may lead on - others will double back on themselves, and others will
come to an abrupt end.
name: Devastation

desc:
The jumble of great cracked stone chunks, masonry blocks, and piles of rubble
continues without end. You cannot see beyond the nearest bend in your miserable
cramped crawlway. Crevices stop short, passages diminish to crannies too narrow
for a rat to squeeze through, slopes become fatal plunges. Your sense of north
and south is completely confused; you can barely tell up from down in this
jumble. The air is warm and very stale, and you seem to smell death in it.
name: Devastation

desc:
This is a thin shell of space surrounding a mass of rock to the east. Unless one
is very short indeed, one must travel by spread-eagling oneself over the rock
and clambering up, down, back, or forth like a spider. The mass of the mountain
stretches above you with no support in sight; the blackness of the crevice
extends around you to the east, north, and south. The crevice also widens below
you, extending a little to the west, but mostly down.
name: In a Crevice

desc:
This is a very irregular and awkward space, created accidentally as part of the
interior of the mountain shifted southward and down, tearing away from the rock
above it and to the north. The cavity remaining is like a thin shell of
emptiness that follows the curving outline of the shifted mass. Nowhere it is as
thick as a man is tall; at its edges it thins and becomes an impassable crack;
and its slopes and dropoffs are unpredictable and dangerous. Here the cavity
slopes noticeably downward to the west and north.
name: In a Crevice

desc:
This chamber is divided in two parts. The eastern part is little more than a
broad and very shallow natural opening between two horizontal sheets of rock.
Not even a dwarf could stand there. The space is not vacant, however - some
process has filled it with crystals of topaz and amethyst in golden and purple
profusion. The western part of the chamber has been expanded in order to allow a
Dwarven audience to enter and gaze upon the beauty of the stones. Lampstands
with brazen reflectors line the western wall.
name: Crystal Garden

desc:
This room has been carefully excavated in the form of a truncated ellipsoid. At
the southern end are ten long, curving benches of stone, arranged to make a sort
of amphitheatre facing northward. Behind the highest and longest bench is a row
of simply designed lampstands, and behind that is a walkway where three passages
enter the room. A person sitting in the amphitheatre is in a position to gaze
into the curved and polished surface of the wall of the north end of the
chamber, where, however, there is (at first glance) nothing to see.
name: Hall of Contemplation

desc:
This is a nearly unimproved passage, actually part of a natural fault in the
rock which has been somewhat widened. The fault actually slopes upward to the
south at a pitch of three to one, so that unless one is very short one cannot
actually stand upright here but must travel while leaning far over to the
southern side. The stone is dark and hard, but small crystalline intrusions
glisten in some of the cracks.
name: Passage along a Fault

desc:
A great dark cavern has been excavated here, through the middle of which, from
north to south, runs an outcropping of white crystalline stone. In fact, this is
a vein, a quartzite intrusion, from which the surrounding rock has been
completely removed, exposing its intricate and branching form. The sheets and
fingers of the stone are in places streaked with other colours - azure, crimson,
lavender. The vein seems to shimmer in the darkness and almost to float in
mid-air - some of the native stone, the minimum possible, has been left to hold
it in place. The walls of the cavern are smooth and unadorned, as if to focus
the undistracted attention of the visitor on the form of the vein.
name: Exposed Vein

desc:
This rock chamber of roughly spherical form was the work neither of dwarves nor
of any other mortals; it has been formed in the heart of the mountain by the
forces of nature under the guidance of the Valar, perhaps of Mahal himself. Its
walls are white, rose, and golden; they are formed of tufts of crystalline
needles, thousands of thousands of them, each more delicate than the finest
thread. It seems as if the slightest breath of air would shatter them all the
way a gust of wind blows away the ripened seeds of a dandelion. And yet such was
the skill of the dwarves that they were able to build a footbridge through this
chamber, from north to south, apparently hanging in mid-air, without disturbing
the delicate beauty around them. The bridge is about three feet in width and has
no railing; at its centre it widens to a circular platform, which is about
twenty feet from the walls of the chamber in each direction.
name: Natural Chamber

desc:
This at first glance the junction of two mine tunnels, the main working gallery
being the one that extends south from here. The floor is not tiled, and the
walls are evenly hewn but unpolished and unfaced. The native rock here is a hard
stone, very dark grey, and the tunnel follows a vein of white quartz. However,
some benches and tables have been installed here, which would seem out of place
in a working mine.
name: Converted Mine Tunnel

desc:
This tunnel, about ten feet in width and in height, ascends evenly toward the
east and descends toward the west. Some runes have been inscribed in the north
and south walls of the tunnel; nothing has marred them in the centuries since
this part of the city was sealed off by the massive earthquakes. All is black
and still; the air is dry and perhaps a little warm.
name: Sloping Passage

desc:
Here a corridor apparently designed for foot traffic meets a sloping east-west
tunnel. The corridor leads southward and upward through an unobstructed doorway.
The tunnel, which resembles an ordinary mine tunnel except for its breadth,
rises gently to the east and descends noticeably to the west.
name: Silent Junction

desc:
Here an unornamented corridor arriving from the north terminates at two
stairways leading south. The one on the right climbs upward a few steps and
terminates at a metal door which is secured by a wheel-like latch. The one on
the left descends into darkness through a small tunnel cut in the stone.
name: Stairway Junction

desc:
This irregularly-shaped vacancy or crevice was left when some of the boulders
and rock slabs in this broken assemblage slipped downward and crashed into the
black void below - something which the rocks above you could do in their turn at
any moment. Which is the original stone of this area, and which is stone which
has fallen in from elsewhere, are impossible to discern.
name: Devastation

desc:
The twists and bends of the crevices through which you clamber have no hint of
pattern or direction to them. Here you seem to be at a lower point; this flat
grey stone here may be the floor of the ill-fated Fourth Deep itself. But that
may be only an illusion. Buried here among the monumental fragments of stone are
scores of corpses, you are sure, maybe hundreds - fierce orc and captive dwarf
alike impersonally crushed and interred. Treasure house and hovel, armoury and
inn, all has been reduced to unrecognisable rubble. The dark gaps around you
seem to mock you; every one seems directionless, pointless, endless.
name: Devastation

desc:
This chamber has been excavated at the point where four rock masses meet. The
northern wall of the chamber is formed of a dark grey stone, and the chamber was
apparently formed mostly by removing stone of that type. The irregular floor
south of the room's centre is formed of a pink marble lined with greenish
striations. A black basaltic intrusion bulges into the room from the southeast,
while in the southwest is a greenish stone, a sort of olivine- rich granite
marked by intricate belts and folds of a darker rock. The effects of colour and
texture where the different types of rock adjoin must have been very interesting
to dwarves, because there are several rows of marble benches and stools (about a
foot high) where short persons could sit and contemplate the southern wall. A
row of lamp-holders lines the northern wall, low down, on either side of the
entrance.
name: Rock Garden

desc:
This simple tunnel, nearly unornamented, slopes noticeably upward to the east
and downward to the west. All around is a warm black stillness; it seems as if
no one has come this way before you in hundreds of years. No side passages open
onto the sloping tunnel in this stretch.
name: Silent Road

desc:
A tunnel, about ten feet wide and with a simple arched cross-section, slopes
downward to the west and disappears into the blackness. Its continuation to the
east has been blocked by a heavy stone door which has been dropped into place
from above. To lift it is beyond the strength of any mortal creature; there is,
or was, probably a mechanism which could do it, but if so it must be on the
other side of the door. The air is very stale here, and a light coating of dust
on the floor is completely undisturbed.
name: Silent Road

desc:
This room houses several large wheeled bins. One contains a heavy brick-red
stone, which yields a reddish dust when scraped. Another contains a light,
black, lustrous stone: hard coal. A third contains white dolomite, which was
used in smelting the iron ore.
name: Ore Bins

desc:
The fragments of the clay chimney of what was once a tall and ingenious smelting
apparatus in the southeast corner of this room lie where they fell after the
quake hundreds of years ago. The ceiling of this ironworks, excavated from the
stone of the mountain, is high and shadowed, and may house a ventilation shaft
which once drew off the hot air. Iron tongs are bolted to the west wall.
name: Ironworks

desc:
While not entirely ruined, this workshop has certainly suffered from the great
earth tremors; the ceiling is riven by cracks, and boulders that have fallen
from it lie everywhere. A long forge lines the east wall, while a huge bellows
made of steel strips and ox-hide occupies the southeast corner, covered with a
layer of gravel. Shelves that contained pans, chains, and pails have been
crushed in the west side of the room.
name: Ruined Smithy

desc:
This was once the vestibule of the residence of a reasonably well-off dwarf. The
quakes that have wrecked so much of the Third Deep have not spared this home
either. All is destruction and darkness. A sculpture, a crystal lamp, an
ornamented runic panel - all the possessions acquired in a lifetime so long ago
are strewn here in shards under and among the fallen stone blocks.
name: Ruined Home

desc:
You are near the southern wall of what was once an east-west underground avenue.
In the heyday of the Dwarrowdelf this was a busy commercial street, brightly lit
and lined by shops. Now it is a dark ruin. The ceiling has fallen in, bringing
down with it the native stone of the mountain; here a slab leaning against the
wall has kept open a sliver of the road, but the stones creak and clatter
ominously as you force your way past.
name: Crawlway along an Avenue

desc:
You are somewhere in the middle of a jumble of fallen rock. A great quake has
brought thousands of tons of stone down into this underground avenue. In places
you can see the pavement, but most of it is hidden by a carpet of stone shards
that clatters ominously as you scramble under and between the boulders.
name: Rockfall

desc:
Only the look and texture of the pavement, where you can see it, gives you any
ready clue that you have left the avenue and arrived at what used to be a
comfortable commercial establishment. The floor is now paved by a mosaic of
small stones, in part of whose pattern you can vaguely make out golden
dinnerware, it seems. Is it possible that anything valuable can still be buried
here? Among the rubble you see crystal shards, and the bones of a small foot
projecting from under a slab...
name: Ruins of a Store
